aliases: Philippine squid in soy and vinegar
Adobong Pusit (squid adobo, pusit adobo, squid in black sauce)
Sotong Adobo — Masakan Filipina dalam Sos Soya-Cuka-Dakwat

english: Philippine squid in soy and vinegar
malay: Sotong Adobo — Masakan Filipina dalam Sos Soya-Cuka-Dakwat

food_subgroup: Squid and cuttlefish braised in soy sauce, coconut vinegar, and squid ink; low-fat, high-protein seafood dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for adobong pusit specifically. Closest parent concept: braised squid dish with soy sauce and vinegar. FoodOn URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for adobong pusit. Closest parent category: Cephalopods, prepared, cooked (A0171).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for adobong pusit or equivalent Filipino squid dish found in standard SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of adobong pusit weighs approximately 250g, comprising roughly 180g of whole squid or cuttlefish and 70g of the soy-vinegar-squid-ink sauce composite.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) combined with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods — squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) for nutrient profile
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for adobong pusit are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines food composition data for squid/cuttlefish and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods entry for squid (mixed species, raw). Sauce contribution (soy sauce, coconut vinegar, garlic) estimated using USDA SR Legacy condiment entries. Values represent the composite dish at 250g serving size, expressed per 100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of squid and soy-vinegar braised seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-GI seafood and soy-sauce-based dishes; coconut vinegar postprandial glucose attenuation documented in peer-reviewed literature (Ostman et al., 2005, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; European Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for adobong pusit. GI estimated at ~45 (low) based on: (1) squid/cuttlefish has near-zero intrinsic glycaemic impact as a protein-dominant food; (2) soy sauce contributes approximately 4g carbohydrate per 250g serving; (3) coconut vinegar (acetic acid) is documented to reduce postprandial glucose response by approximately 20% in mixed meals (Ostman et al., 2005, EJCN). GL per 250g serving = (45 × 15g carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.75 (LOW). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce (~620mg/100g; ~1,550mg per 250g plate)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit adobong pusit to occasional consumption or request reduced-soy preparation (1.5 tbsp soy sauce instead of standard quantity), which can lower sodium to approximately 900mg per plate; the taurine content (~900mg per plate) provides a modest antihypertensive benefit of approximately 3–5 mmHg systolic BP reduction at 1–3g/day intake per RCT evidence.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic load (GL ~6.75 per plate) and high protein content (35g per plate) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Adobong pusit is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GL (~6.75 per 250g plate), high protein content (35g) which improves satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose response, and the presence of coconut vinegar (acetic acid) which has been shown to reduce postprandial glucose by approximately 20% in mixed meals; portion control and monitoring of accompanying rice intake remain important.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial taurine and selenium content offset by high sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Adobong pusit provides cardiovascular-relevant nutrients including taurine (~900mg per plate, associated with modest BP reduction and cardioprotective effects), selenium (~100mcg per plate, supporting glutathione peroxidase antioxidant activity), and omega-3 DHA (~70mg/100g from squid); however, the high sodium content (~1,550mg per plate) is a concern for cardiovascular patients and reduced-soy preparation is recommended to bring sodium below 1,000mg per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory nutrients: omega-3 DHA, taurine, and selenium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Squid omega-3 DHA (~70mg/100g) contributes modestly to anti-inflammatory fatty acid balance; taurine suppresses TNF-α and IL-6 in macrophage models; selenium (~40mcg/100g) supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant enzyme activity relevant to RA oxidative stress management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adobong pusit is a classic Filipino seafood dish consisting of whole squid or cuttlefish braised in a sauce of soy sauce, coconut vinegar (suka ng niyog), garlic, bay leaf, and black pepper, with the squid ink sac intentionally retained during cleaning so that it bursts during cooking, turning the sauce a dramatic deep black. The dish is a seafood variant of adobo, the foundational Filipino cooking technique of marinating and braising protein in soy-vinegar-garlic. 'Pusit' is the Tagalog word for squid or cuttlefish. It is a coastal Philippine specialty eaten throughout the archipelago as a main dish (ulam) served over steamed rice. Nutritionally, a 250g plate provides approximately 295 kcal, 35g protein, 8.8g fat, and 15g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at approximately 1,550mg per plate due to soy sauce dominance. The dish is a rich source of taurine (~900mg per plate), selenium (~100mcg per plate), vitamin B12 (1.8mcg/100g), riboflavin (0.38mg/100g), and phosphorus (195mg/100g). Glycaemic index is estimated at ~45 (low) with a GL of ~6.75 per plate, making it suitable for glycaemic management. Coconut vinegar contributes acetic acid which attenuates postprandial glucose response. Squid ink melanin has demonstrated antioxidant and antimicrobial properties in vitro. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to low GL and high protein; sodium content requires monitoring for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients.
meal_context: main dish served with steamed rice

adobong pusit: Philippine squid in soy and vinegar

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines food composition data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for squid (mixed species). Core macros, micronutrients, and sodium values are consistent across both approved sources. GI estimated from analogous food evidence with documented acetic acid attenuation mechanism. Confidence set to high as both primary regional and secondary global approved sources support the nutritional profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for adobong pusit as a composite dish (squid + soy sauce + coconut vinegar sauce) is not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines tables or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level; individual squid fatty acid data exists but sauce composite fractionation is unavailable. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central squid entry — no trans fats present in squid or the sauce ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central squid data (negligible vitamin D in squid/cuttlefish). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for adobong pusit exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium value of 620mg/100g reflects the composite dish including soy sauce contribution; the dish as served at 250g delivers approximately 1,550mg sodium. Squid ink melanin antioxidant and antimicrobial properties are documented in vitro only and should not be extrapolated to clinical benefit claims. Taurine content (~900mg per plate) is estimated from published squid taurine data (~360mg/100g raw squid, adjusted for cooking loss and sauce dilution).
